title: World leaders gather in Geneva as climate talks reach critical juncture
desc: Representatives from over 140 nations have assembled for what analysts are calling the most consequential environmental summit in a decade, with binding emissions targets at stake.
content:
GENEVA — World leaders arrived in Geneva on Sunday for emergency climate talks that diplomats say could determine the trajectory of global emissions policy for the next three decades.

Representatives from over 140 nations have assembled for what analysts are calling the most consequential environmental summit in a decade. At the heart of the negotiations are binding emissions targets, green energy transition timelines, and a proposed $500 billion climate fund for developing nations.

The summit comes amid a string of record-breaking temperature anomalies reported across four continents this year, lending urgency to the proceedings. Scientists from the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change warned delegates in an opening briefing that without immediate action, average global temperatures could exceed 1.8°C above pre-industrial levels by 2045.

"This is not a photo opportunity," said one senior European delegate. "We leave here with a deal or we leave having failed the next generation."

Negotiations are expected to continue through the week, with a final communiqué scheduled for Friday.
author: Sarah Okonkwo

title: Central bank holds interest rates steady amid easing inflation pressures
desc: Policymakers cite easing price pressures but warn that premature cuts could reignite inflationary trends seen earlier this decade.
content:
The central bank held its benchmark interest rate steady at 4.75% on Thursday, citing progress on inflation but signalling caution about moving too quickly toward rate cuts.

The decision, which was unanimous among the rate-setting committee, came despite growing calls from business groups and some economists for an immediate reduction to stimulate sluggish economic growth.

In a statement, the bank's governor noted that headline inflation had fallen to 2.4% — its lowest level in three years — but warned that services inflation remained elevated and that global supply chain disruptions posed ongoing upside risks.

"We are encouraged by the progress made," the governor said at a press conference. "But we must be certain that inflation is durably returning to target before easing policy."

Markets had priced in a roughly 30% chance of a cut at this meeting. Following the decision, bond yields edged higher and the currency strengthened slightly against major peers.

The next rate decision is scheduled for June 12.
author: James Whitfield

title: Parliament debates sweeping electoral reform bill ahead of autumn session
desc: The proposed legislation would overhaul constituency boundaries and introduce ranked-choice voting for the first time in the country's history.
content:
Parliament erupted into heated debate on Wednesday as lawmakers began scrutinising a sweeping electoral reform bill that its sponsors say would modernise the country's democratic system.

The proposed legislation, tabled by the government's junior coalition partner, would redraw constituency boundaries for the first time in 22 years and introduce ranked-choice voting for general elections — a change critics say could fundamentally alter the balance of political power.

Opposition leaders condemned the bill as a transparent attempt to entrench the ruling coalition's advantage, while civil society groups offered a more nuanced assessment, praising the ranked-choice provisions while questioning the boundary-drawing methodology.

"Every vote should count equally," said the bill's lead sponsor. "Our current system was designed for a different era."

The bill faces a difficult path through the upper chamber, where the government lacks a majority. Observers expect months of negotiation before any final vote.
author: Fatima Al-Rashid

title: Researchers unveil solid-state battery that charges in under four minutes
desc: The breakthrough could revolutionise electric vehicles and consumer electronics, with commercial production expected by 2028.
content:
Scientists at a leading research university announced a major breakthrough in battery technology on Tuesday, unveiling a solid-state cell capable of charging to full capacity in under four minutes while retaining 95% of its charge capacity after 5,000 cycles.

The development, published in the journal Nature Energy, represents a significant advance over current lithium-ion batteries, which typically require 30 to 60 minutes for a full charge and degrade noticeably after 500 to 1,000 cycles.

The key innovation lies in a novel ceramic electrolyte that allows ions to move far more quickly than in conventional designs, while also eliminating the fire risk associated with liquid electrolytes.

Electric vehicle manufacturers responded enthusiastically to the announcement. Two major automakers confirmed they had entered into licensing discussions with the research team.

Commercial production of the new cells is not expected before 2028, and analysts cautioned that scaling laboratory results to mass manufacturing remains a substantial engineering challenge.
author: Dr. Priya Nair

title: National team secures World Cup qualification after dramatic comeback
desc: Two late goals turned the match on its head as the squad booked their place in next year's tournament with a game to spare.
content:
The national football team secured their place at next year's World Cup with one of the most dramatic performances in recent memory, overturning a two-goal deficit in the final fifteen minutes to win 3–2 against a determined opponent.

A packed stadium witnessed scenes of jubilation as the final whistle confirmed qualification, with players and staff embracing on the pitch and thousands of fans celebrating in the streets outside.

The victory was built on resilience rather than dominance. The home side fell behind after two defensive errors in the first half and looked set for a damaging defeat until a moment of individual brilliance changed the match's momentum.

"We never stopped believing," the head coach said afterwards. "That's the character of this team."

The squad now has one remaining qualification match, which the coach confirmed will be used to rest senior players and give younger squad members competitive experience ahead of the tournament.
author: Marcus Obi

title: New exhibition brings century-old photographs of daily life to the public
desc: Over 300 recently restored images offer a rare window into ordinary life from a hundred years ago.
content:
A new photography exhibition opening this weekend offers visitors an intimate look at daily life from a century ago, featuring over 300 restored images drawn from private collections and regional archives.

The exhibition, titled Ordinary Days, spans decades of the early twentieth century and covers subjects ranging from market days and schoolyards to factory floors and family gatherings.

Many of the photographs had not been publicly displayed before and were painstakingly digitised and restored by a team of archivists over three years.

"There is something quietly radical about showing ordinary people living ordinary lives," the exhibition's curator said at a preview event. "History is usually told through great events. This is history told through Tuesday mornings."

Ordinary Days opens on Saturday and runs through August. Admission is free on weekdays.
author: Leila Morrow
